 {"id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.9","title":"[PERF] Reproducible startup profiling workflow (gprofng fallback)","description":"# Objective

Make the profiling step repeatable so future performance work can reliably identify hotspots **before** implementing changes.

# Why

Host environments may block `perf` (e.g., `perf_event_paranoid=4`). We already successfully used `gprofng` to profile dcg startup/pack listing.

This bead codifies:
- exact commands
- how to interpret outputs
- where to store artifacts

# Desired workflow

## CPU profile (startup-heavy subcommand)
- Use a loop to amplify cost and stabilize measurements.
- Prefer `dcg packs --enabled` as the reproducer for registry init.

Example (adapt as needed):
- `gprofng collect app -o /tmp/dcg_packs_loop_cpu.er -F '=dcg' -p on sh -c 'for i in $(seq 1 20); do ./target/debug/dcg packs --enabled >/dev/null; done'`
- `gprofng display text -functions /tmp/dcg_packs_loop_cpu.er | head -n 50`

## Heap profile
- `gprofng collect app -o /tmp/dcg_packs_heap.er -H on -p on ./target/debug/dcg packs --enabled >/dev/null`

## I/O sanity
If gprofng I/O init fails, use:
- `strace -c -f ./target/release/dcg packs --enabled >/dev/null`

# Deliverables

- A short doc snippet (in-repo or in a bead comment) that records:
  - profiling commands
  - common failure modes (perf blocked)
  - how to compare before/after
- Optional helper script under `scripts/` (only if it adds real value and doesn’t create duplication)

# Acceptance criteria

- Anyone can reproduce CPU hotspot identification in <5 minutes.
- Output clearly shows top functions by inclusive time.

- Run the profiling workflow on a release binary and capture commands, environment, and outputs as artifacts.
- Cross-check against perf harness baselines; record any deviations with logs.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T16:21:56.832215971-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-10T18:44:41.668490024-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-10T18:44:41.668490024-05:00","close_reason":"Documented gprofng profiling workflow in design-lazy-pack-registry","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.9","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-09T16:21:56.833710647-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-64n","title":"Replace remaining mocks/fakes with real integration fixtures","description":"## Objective

Remove any residual mocked/fake behaviors in tests outside evaluator by shifting to real fixtures/integration-style tests.

## Scope

- Survey tests for stubs/fakes (beyond evaluator).
- Convert to real filesystem fixtures (temp dirs), real hook input parsing, real pack matching.
- Prefer integration tests in `tests/` when the unit boundary becomes artificial.

## Deliverables

- Updated tests that use real data and real code paths (no mocks).
- Rationale for any remaining mocks (if absolutely required).

## Acceptance criteria

- Mock/fake usage reduced to near-zero for core pipeline tests.
- New tests are deterministic and fast (no external network).

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-67ff","title":"[E2-T8] Update denial output to display explanations","description":"## Task: Update denial output to display explanations

### Parent Epic
Epic 2: Natural Language Explanations (git_safety_guard-t01x)

### What To Do
Modify src/output/denial.rs to prominently display pattern explanations when a command is blocked.

### Implementation Details
1. Check if matched pattern has explanation
2. Display explanation in denial box:
   ```
   ╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
   │  🛑 BLOCKED                                                      │
   ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
   │                                                                  │
   │  This command would permanently delete all files and             │
   │  subdirectories in the target path without confirmation.         │
   │  If the path is incorrect, data loss is unrecoverable.          │
   │                                                                  │
   │  Pattern: filesystem.recursive_delete                            │
   │  Pack: core (severity: critical)                                 │
   ╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
   ```

3. Word-wrap explanation to fit terminal width
4. Style explanation differently from pattern name (more prominent)
5. Graceful fallback if no explanation (show pattern name only)

### Files to Modify
- src/output/denial.rs
- src/output/theme.rs (if new styles needed)

### Dependencies
- E2-T1 (explanation field must exist)
- E2-T2 through E2-T7 (nice to have explanations to display)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T14:06:42.309314935-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T14:06:42.309314935-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-67ff","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-q6f7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T14:15:36.569037341-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}

### Parent Epic
Epic 3: Command Rewriting Suggestions (git_safety_guard-96c8)

### What To Do
Extend the Pattern struct to include an optional list of safer command alternatives.

### Implementation Details
1. Create Suggestion struct:
   ```rust
   pub struct Suggestion {
       pub command: String,        // The safer command
       pub description: String,    // Why it's safer
       pub platform: Option<Platform>, // None = all platforms
   }
   
   pub enum Platform {
       Linux,
       MacOS,
       Windows,
       All,
   }
   ```

2. Add to Pattern struct:
   ```rust
   pub struct Pattern {
       // existing fields...
       pub suggestions: Vec<Suggestion>,
   }
   ```

3. Update pattern! macro:
   ```rust
   pattern!(
       id: "filesystem.recursive_delete",
       suggestions: [
           ("rm -ri {path}", "Interactive mode prompts for each file"),
           ("trash {path}", "Moves to trash instead of permanent deletion", Platform::Linux),
       ]
   )
   ```

### Files to Modify
- src/packs/mod.rs

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-69d7","title":"[E7-T7] Comprehensive testing for Interactive Learning Mode","description":"## Comprehensive Unit & E2E Testing for Epic 7: Interactive Learning Mode

### Overview
Testing for the interactive mode where users can press a key to add blocked commands to allowlist with security safeguards.

### Unit Tests Required

#### 1. TTY Detection Tests (tests/unit/test_tty_detection.rs)
```rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use std::io::IsTerminal;
    
    #[test]
    fn test_detects_interactive_terminal() {
        // Mock terminal detection
        let result = is_interactive_session();
        // In test environment, typically not a TTY
        assert!(!result); // Verify it correctly detects non-TTY
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn test_ci_environment_detection() {
        // CI environment should never be interactive
        std::env::set_var("CI", "true");
        assert!(!is_interactive_session());
        std::env::remove_var("CI");
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn test_dumb_terminal_detection() {
        std::env::set_var("TERM", "dumb");
        assert!(!is_interactive_session());
        std::env::remove_var("TERM");
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn test_interactive_mode_disabled_in_pipe() {
        // When stdout is piped, should not be interactive
        // This is architecture for testing, not a direct unit test
    }
}
```

#### 2. Security Safeguard Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_learning_mode_requires_explicit_opt_in() {
    // Learning mode should be OFF by default
    let config = Config::default();
    assert!(!config.learning_mode_enabled);
}

#[test]
fn test_critical_severity_cannot_be_learned() {
    let result = can_learn_pattern("rm -rf /", Severity::Critical);
    assert!(!result);
}

#[test]
fn test_learning_mode_disabled_on_protected_branches() {
    // On main/master, learning should be disabled
    let result = can_learn_in_context(Context { branch: "main" });
    assert!(!result);
}

#[test]
fn test_confirmation_required_for_high_severity() {
    // P1/P2 patterns should require explicit confirmation
    let result = requires_confirmation(Severity::High);
    assert!(result);
}
```

#### 3. Allowlist Entry Generation Tests
- test_learned_entry_includes_context
- test_learned_entry_is_scoped_to_directory
- test_learned_entry_has_expiration_option
- test_generated_entry_syntax_is_valid

### Integration Tests (tests/integration/interactive_learning.rs)

```rust
#[test]
fn test_learning_prompt_appears_in_terminal() {
    // Use PTY to simulate terminal
    let pty = spawn_dcg_in_pty(&["check", "blocked-command"]);
    let output = pty.read_until_prompt();
    assert!(output.contains("Add to allowlist?") || output.contains("[a]llow"));
}

#[test]
fn test_learning_does_not_appear_in_ci() {
    std::env::set_var("CI", "true");
    let output = run_dcg_check("blocked-command");
    assert!(!output.contains("Add to allowlist?"));
    std::env::remove_var("CI");
}

#[test]
fn test_keypress_adds_to_allowlist() {
    let pty = spawn_dcg_in_pty(&["check", "npm run custom"]);
    pty.send_key('a'); // Press 'a' to allow
    
    // Verify entry was added
    let config = read_config();
    assert!(config.allowlist.contains("npm run custom"));
}
```

### E2E Test Scenarios (tests/e2e/interactive/)

#### Scenario 1: Interactive Learning E2E (test_interactive.sh)
```bash
#!/bin/bash
LOG_FILE="${TEST_LOG_DIR}/interactive_$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S).log"
log() { echo "[$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')] $*" | tee -a "$LOG_FILE"; }

log "=== E2E Test: Interactive Learning Mode ==="

# This test requires PTY simulation - use expect or similar
if ! command -v expect &> /dev/null; then
    log "SKIP: expect not installed, cannot test interactive mode"
    exit 0
fi

TEMP_DIR=$(mktemp -d)
export DCG_CONFIG="$TEMP_DIR/.dcg.toml"

# Enable learning mode
cat > "$DCG_CONFIG" << 'EOF'
[settings]
learning_mode = true
EOF

log "Step 1: Run dcg check in interactive mode"
expect << 'EXPECT_SCRIPT'
spawn dcg check "npm run custom-script"
expect {
    "Add to allowlist" {
        send "a"
        exp_continue
    }
    "Added to allowlist" {
        exit 0
    }
    timeout {
        exit 1
    }
}
EXPECT_SCRIPT

if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    log "PASS: Interactive learning completed"
else
    log "FAIL: Interactive learning did not complete"
    exit 1
fi

log "Step 2: Verify entry in config"
if grep -q "npm run custom-script" "$DCG_CONFIG"; then
    log "PASS: Entry added to config"
else
    log "FAIL: Entry not found in config"
    exit 1
fi

rm -rf "$TEMP_DIR"
log "=== Interactive learning test passed ==="
```

#### Scenario 2: Security Safeguard Testing
- Test that critical commands show warning even with learning mode
- Test that protected branch detection works
- Test rate limiting on learning operations

#### Scenario 3: Non-Interactive Fallback
- Test graceful fallback when not in TTY
- Test CI environment detection
- Test piped input/output handling

### Test Fixtures
- tests/fixtures/interactive_config.toml - Config with learning mode
- tests/fixtures/expect_scripts/ - Expect scripts for PTY testing

### Logging Requirements
- Log TTY detection results
- Log security check results (why learning was allowed/blocked)
- Log keypress events and responses
- Log config file modifications

### Success Criteria
- [ ] TTY detection works correctly
- [ ] CI environments never get interactive prompts
- [ ] Critical severity patterns cannot be learned
- [ ] Protected branch detection works
- [ ] Learning creates correctly formatted allowlist entries
- [ ] Graceful degradation when not interactive

### Dependencies

- Depends on: E7-T1 through E7-T6 (implementation)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T14:51:54.832531393-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T14:51:54.832531393-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-69d7","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-v2ti","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T14:54:07.118557244-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-69d7","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-qnci","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T14:55:23.211175298-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-6ao","title":"Feature: Codecov Cloud Integration for Coverage Trend Tracking","description":"# Feature: Codecov Cloud Integration

## Background

DCG currently generates coverage reports using `cargo-llvm-cov` and stores them as CI artifacts. While this provides point-in-time coverage data, it lacks:

1. **Historical trend tracking** - Can't see if coverage is improving or declining over time
2. **PR coverage diffs** - No automatic comments showing what lines a PR adds/removes from coverage
3. **Coverage badges** - No README badge showing current coverage status
4. **Cross-branch comparison** - Can't compare feature branch coverage against main

CASS (coding_agent_session_search) has Codecov integration that provides all of these. This feature brings DCG to parity.

## Why This Matters for DCG

DCG enforces strict coverage thresholds:
- 70% overall minimum
- 80% for src/evaluator.rs (core decision logic)
- 80% for src/hook.rs (output formatting)

These thresholds are enforced but not tracked over time. With Codecov:
- We can see coverage trends (are we improving?)
- PRs automatically show coverage impact
- New code that drops coverage is immediately visible
- Team can set coverage targets and track progress

## Technical Approach

### Codecov Setup
1. Codecov is free for open source repositories
2. No token required for public repos (DCG is public)
3. Uses GitHub App integration for PR comments

### CI Changes
Modify `.github/workflows/ci.yml` coverage job:

```yaml
- name: Upload to Codecov
  uses: codecov/codecov-action@v4
  with:
    files: lcov.info
    fail_ci_if_error: false  # Don't fail build if upload fails
    verbose: true
```

### Coverage Configuration
Create `codecov.yml` in repo root:

```yaml
coverage:
  status:
    project:
      default:
        target: 70%
        threshold: 2%
    patch:
      default:
        target: 80%  # New code should be well-tested

comment:
  layout: "reach,diff,flags,tree"
  behavior: default
  require_changes: true

ignore:
  - "tests/**"
  - "benches/**"
  - "fuzz/**"
```

## Implementation Tasks

1. **Add Codecov upload to CI** - Modify coverage job
2. **Create codecov.yml config** - Define coverage targets and behavior
3. **Add coverage badge to README** - Show current status
4. **Verify PR comments work** - Test on a real PR

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Coverage uploads to Codecov on every push/PR
- [ ] PR comments show coverage diff
- [ ] README displays coverage badge
- [ ] Codecov dashboard shows historical trends
- [ ] No CI failures from Codecov (fail_ci_if_error: false)

## Considerations

- **Token**: Not required for public repos, but can add CODECOV_TOKEN secret for reliability
- **Coverage format**: Using LCOV (lcov.info) which Codecov supports natively
- **Existing thresholds**: Keep our enforced thresholds in CI; Codecov is additive visibility
- **Privacy**: Coverage data is public for public repos - this is fine for DCG

## References

- Codecov GitHub Action: https://github.com/codecov/codecov-action
- CASS implementation: /data/projects/coding_agent_session_search/.github/workflows/coverage.yml","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:20:04.545615215-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-11T10:17:08.4300477-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-11T10:17:08.4300477-05:00","close_reason":"All 4 prerequisites complete: codecov.yml config, CI upload step, e2e verification, and README badge.  {"id":"git_safety_guard-po7","title":"[PACK] cicd.jenkins - Jenkins Pack","description":"# Jenkins Pack

## Scope
Implement pattern matching for Jenkins CLI and API operations.

## Destructive Patterns
- `jenkins-cli delete-job` - Deletes jobs
- `jenkins-cli delete-node` - Deletes build nodes
- `jenkins-cli delete-credentials` - Deletes credentials
- `jenkins-cli delete-builds` - Deletes build history
- `curl -X POST .../doDelete` - REST API deletion

## Safe Patterns
- `jenkins-cli list-jobs`
- `jenkins-cli get-job`
- `jenkins-cli build` (trigger)
- `jenkins-cli who-am-i`
- `curl` GET requests to Jenkins

## Implementation Notes
- Parent category: cicd.* (git_safety_guard-0v8)
- Keywords: jenkins-cli
- Note: Many orgs use custom Jenkins CLI wrappers

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Job deletion blocked
- [ ] Credential deletion blocked
- [ ] Node operations handled
- [ ] REST API patterns detected","notes":"Testing/Validation:
- Add unit tests for each destructive pattern plus safe/benign commands (false-positive cases).
- Add e2e corpus entries in the shared harness with verbose logging; ensure pack_id/pattern_name appear in logs.
- Use the pack unit test template (git_safety_guard-i79) and logging infra (git_safety_guard-jdb).","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:31:28.246062425-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-10T17:47:06.591230026-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-10T17:47:06.591230026-05:00","close_reason":"Added Jenkins CLI/API pack with delete patterns, safe patterns, tests, registry updates","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-po7","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ikf2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:33.817663429-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-po7","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-i79","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:33.858089301-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-po7","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ct5s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:33.898414153-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-po7","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-jdb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:33.937991368-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-po7","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-e7j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:33.97802926-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-po7","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-l968","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:34.016856364-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-po7","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-qxc7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:09.674097567-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-po7","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-6ozg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:09.7204562-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-pwfy","title":"[E4-T7] Implement history bypass pattern analyzer","description":"## Implement History Bypass Pattern Analyzer

### Goal
Analyze the history database to identify commands that are frequently bypassed, indicating they may be candidates for permanent allowlisting.

### Implementation Details

#### Query Design
```sql
SELECT command, 
       COUNT(*) as bypass_count,
       MAX(timestamp) as last_bypass,
       rule_id,
       pattern_name
FROM command_history 
WHERE outcome = 'bypass'
GROUP BY command 
HAVING bypass_count >= 3
ORDER BY bypass_count DESC
```

#### Rust Module: src/analysis/bypass_patterns.rs
```rust
pub struct BypassPattern {
    pub command: String,
    pub bypass_count: usize,
    pub last_bypass: DateTime<Utc>,
    pub rule_id: Option<String>,
    pub pattern_name: Option<String>,
    pub confidence_score: f64,
}

pub fn analyze_bypass_patterns(db: &HistoryDb, threshold: usize) -> Vec<BypassPattern>;
```

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Query returns commands with 3+ bypasses
- [ ] Results include rule/pattern metadata
- [ ] Confidence score calculated based on frequency and recency
- [ ] Handles empty history gracefully

- src/history/queries.rs (new or extend)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T14:56:10.067910743-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T15:09:03.378926456-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-pwfy","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-olev","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T15:11:54.186624288-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-pwsr","title":"[Epic 9] Agent-Specific Profiles","description":"## EPIC 9: Agent-Specific Profiles

### Background & Context
dcg is explicitly designed for AI coding agents (Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Aider, Continue, Codex CLI). Different agents have:
- Different trust levels (established vs new)
- Different behavior patterns (some clean temp files often)
- Different risk profiles

One-size-fits-all policy is suboptimal. A trusted, well-known agent like Claude Code might deserve more trust than an unknown agent.

### Goals & Objectives
1. Detect which AI agent is invoking dcg
2. Configure per-agent strictness and allowlists
3. Support progressive trust (new agents start restricted)
4. Document agent detection for supported agents

### Reasoning & Justification
This is ranked #9 because it matches dcg's CORE USE CASE:
- dcg exists for AI agents - agent awareness is natural
- Different agents genuinely have different risk profiles
- Enables organizational policy (only approved agents get trust)
- Allows per-agent tuning without global changes

### Config Schema Design
[agents.claude-code]
trust_level = "high"
disabled_packs = ["filesystem.temp_cleanup"]
additional_allowlist = ["npm run build", "cargo build"]

[agents.aider]
trust_level = "medium"
# Uses defaults

[agents.unknown]
trust_level = "low"
extra_packs = ["paranoid"]

### Trust Levels
- "high": Relaxed checking, more allowances
- "medium": Standard checking (default)
- "low": Extra strict, paranoid pack enabled

### Agent Detection Methods
1. Environment variables:
   - CLAUDE_CODE=1
   - AIDER_SESSION=1
   - CONTINUE_SESSION_ID
   - CODEX_CLI=1
2. Parent process inspection (check process tree)
3. Explicit --agent=<name> flag
4. Hook configuration context

### Implementation Considerations
- Agent detection in src/cli.rs or dedicated module
- Profile merging: agent config overlays base config
- Clear precedence: agent allowlist + base allowlist
- Show detected agent in verbose/debug output
- Document detection for each supported agent
- Fallback to 'unknown' profile if not detected

### Success Criteria
- Correct agent detection for all supported agents
- Per-agent config works as expected
- Unknown agents get restricted treatment
- Clear documentation for users

### Related Codebase Files
- src/cli.rs or src/agent.rs (agent detection)
- src/config.rs (agent profiles)
- src/evaluator.rs (profile application)

### Dependencies
- None - independent infrastructure

### Project Goal Alignment
This serves the 'robust, reliable' goals by acknowledging that dcg's primary users are AI agents, and different agents deserve different treatment. It's dog-fooding dcg's own use case.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T13:09:38.45999796-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T13:58:45.103505696-05:00"}

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-qb4b","title":"[PACK] cloud.gcp: container registry destructive patterns","description":"# GCP container registry gaps\n\n## Scope\nAdd destructive patterns to cloud.gcp pack for container registry operations.\n\n## Destructive patterns (examples)\n- gcloud container images delete\n- gcloud artifacts docker images delete\n- gcloud artifacts repositories delete\n\n## Acceptance criteria\n- [ ] Patterns added with reasons + tests\n- [ ] Keywords updated if needed (artifacts, images)\n- [ ] E2E pack cases added","notes":"Updates in src/packs/cloud/gcp.rs: added keywords (artifacts/images/repositories) and destructive patterns for gcloud container images delete, gcloud artifacts docker images delete, and gcloud artifacts repositories delete; added unit tests.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-10T16:59:51.046413564-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-10T17:39:50.629784403-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-10T17:39:50.629784403-05:00","close_reason":"Patterns + tests added"}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-qdhh","title":"[DESIGN] GitHub/GitLab Pack Scope Delineation","description":"# GitHub/GitLab Pack Scope Delineation

## Problem
We have overlapping packs that both cover `gh` and `glab` commands:
- `platform.github` / `platform.gitlab` - Broad platform operations
- `cicd.github_actions` / `cicd.gitlab_ci` - CI/CD specific operations

## Proposed Resolution

### Merge Approach (RECOMMENDED)
Merge the platform and cicd packs for each provider:
- `github.*` single pack covering all `gh` commands
- `gitlab.*` single pack covering all `glab` commands

**Rationale**: 
- Users think in terms of 'GitHub commands' not 'platform vs CI'
- Simpler mental model
- No risk of pattern duplication or gaps
- Easier to maintain

### Implementation
1. Close `platform.github` (git_safety_guard-dxs) and `platform.gitlab` (git_safety_guard-h5j) categories
2. Close `platform.github` (git_safety_guard-3w5) and `platform.gitlab` (git_safety_guard-z2q) pack tasks
3. Rename `cicd.github_actions` to `github` and expand scope
4. Rename `cicd.gitlab_ci` to `gitlab` and expand scope
5. Move expanded packs to appropriate category (or create new `vcs.*` category)

## Decision Required
- [ ] Team agrees on merge approach
- [ ] Update affected beads
- [ ] Document in architecture decisions

## Dependencies
- Blocks: Implementation of any GitHub/GitLab packs","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:33:33.787725725-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-09T16:03:57.151825718-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-09T16:03:57.151825718-05:00","close_reason":"DECIDED: Keep packs separate but clarify boundaries. cicd.github_actions covers CI operations (secrets, variables, workflows, runs). platform.github covers platform operations (repos, releases, deploy keys, webhooks, collaborators). Updated platform.github pack description to explicitly list what's NOT in cicd pack."}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-qdw8","title":"[E4-T8] Implement pattern generalization for similar commands","description":"## Implement Pattern Generalization

### Goal
When multiple similar commands are bypassed, generalize them into a single glob/regex pattern for the allowlist.

### Implementation Details

#### Algorithm
1. Group bypassed commands by common prefix
2. Identify variable suffixes (build:dev, build:prod → build:*)
3. Detect path variations (/tmp/cache1, /tmp/cache2 → /tmp/cache*)
4. Generate minimal pattern that matches all variants

#### Rust Module: src/analysis/pattern_generalization.rs
```rust
pub fn generalize_commands(commands: &[String]) -> Option<GeneralizedPattern> {
    // Find longest common prefix
    // Identify variable parts
    // Generate glob pattern
}

pub struct GeneralizedPattern {
    pub pattern: String,
    pub matched_commands: Vec<String>,
    pub confidence: f64,
}
```

### Edge Cases
- Single command (no generalization possible)
- Commands with no common structure
- Commands with sensitive paths (should not wildcard)

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Generalizes npm run build:* variants
- [ ] Generalizes path-based variations
- [ ] Does not over-generalize (too broad patterns)
- [ ] Confidence reflects pattern specificity","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T14:56:11.991158765-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T15:09:03.455100409-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-qdw8","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-pwfy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T14:59:21.051285626-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}
